column1: A whole slide image of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma at low resolution with visually descriptive characteristics of a well-demarcated, solid mass, typically with a uniform tan or beige cut surface. Compared to clear cell RCC, necrosis and cystic changes are less common. The tumor's growth is mostly expansive, pushing against the surrounding renal tissue with relatively smooth borders.

A whole slide image of clear cell renal cell carcinoma at low resolution with visually descriptive characteristics of irregular or lobulated tumor regions, often with prominent necrosis and cystic changes. The tumor typically appears golden-yellow or orange (due to high lipid and glycogen content) and its growth pushes against and invades the surrounding kidney tissue, causing distortion or displacement of adjacent normal parenchymal structures. The tumor margins are usually irregular, indicating an infiltrative growth pattern.

A whole slide image of papillary renal cell carcinoma at low resolution with visually descriptive characteristics of a generally well-demarcated round or nodular mass, which can also be multifocal. The tumor often exhibits hemorrhage and hemosiderin deposition, giving the cut surface a brown or variegated appearance. The tumor's growth pattern may lead to the compression of surrounding kidney tissue, but it is often less invasive than clear cell RCC.

A whole slide image of chromophobe renal cell carcinoma at high resolution with visually descriptive characteristics of large, polygonal cells arranged in solid sheets. The most classic features are abundant, eosinophilic, granular or flocculent cytoplasm and very distinct cell borders, creating a "plant-cell-like" appearance. Nuclei are often irregular with wrinkled contours ("raisinoid" nuclei) and frequently exhibit a perinuclear halo. While blood vessels are present, they are not as richly networked as in clear cell RCC.

A whole slide image of clear cell renal cell carcinoma at high resolution with visually descriptive characteristics of clear or slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm, resulting from abundant intracellular glycogen and lipids that are washed out during routine processing. Cell borders are distinct, and the cells are polygonal, often arranged in solid sheets, acini, or nests. A highly rich vascular network is characteristic, featuring a delicate, "chicken-wire" pattern of thin-walled blood vessels separating the cell nests. Nuclei are typically round, and the nuclear grade (Fuhrman or ISUP grade) can range from low to high, with high-grade tumors showing prominent nucleoli and irregular nuclear contours.

A whole slide image of papillary renal cell carcinoma at high resolution with its most prominent feature being cells arranged around fibrovascular cores, forming papillary or tubulo-papillary structures. It can be classified into two types based on cell morphology: Type 1 consists of smaller, basophilic cells with scant cytoplasm, arranged in a single layer along the papillary cores. Type 2 features larger cells with eosinophilic, granular cytoplasm, prominent nucleoli, and often pseudostratified nuclei. Foamy histiocytes (macrophages) are frequently seen within the stroma of the papillary structures.
